steps.title: Identify what the nouns are doing
steps.content: "Rock" and "rap" are two genres, and each has its own chorus-to-verse ratio. The sentence even says the ratios "trended similarly, with each genre's data indicating" a separate ratio per genre, so possession is separate, not joint.

steps.title: Apply the separate-possession rule
steps.content: When two singular nouns each independently possess their own version of the following noun, standard English marks both with apostrophe-s: "rock's and rap's chorus-to-verse ratios." The genre names are singular (not "rocks" or "raps").

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'rocks and raps': pluralizes the genre names and shows no possession, but the ratios belong to the genres. ✗
  • Choice B — "rock's and rap's": correctly marks both singular genre names as possessives, matching the separate per-genre ratios. ✓
  • Choice C — "rocks and rap's": combines a plural noun with a possessive, which is ungrammatical. ✗
  • Choice D — "rock and rap's": marks possession only once, which signals joint ownership of a single shared ratio, but the sentence describes a separate ratio for each genre. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
